commands.repair_reason: - [P1] Needs maintainer review for the fail-closed compatibility tradeoff and contributor real behavior proof; there is no narrow automated code repair to request.; Cleared: The diff reduces an untrusted Signal container response memory-exhaustion surface and does not add new dependencies, CI, secrets handling, or supply-chain execution paths. commands.review_summary:
The PR changes Signal container REST success and error body reads to use bounded SDK readers and adds focused tests for oversized success and error responses.

PR surface: Source +9, Tests +68. Total +77 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue? Source-reproducible yes: current main's `containerRestRequest` uses `res.text()` on both error and success paths, and the PR's mocked stream tests show the oversized-body failure mode, but I did not run a live container repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Needs maintainer review for the fail-closed compatibility tradeoff and contributor real behavior proof; there is no narrow automated code repair to request.

target.title: fix(signal): cap container REST responses so a hostile signal-cli-rest-api host cannot exhaust memory
target.body:
_AI-assisted: implemented with Claude Code (Anthropic). Degree: AI wrote the code change and tests under human direction; a human reviewed the diff, ran the focused test suite and type-check locally, and can explain each line. Session prompt: bound the central Signal container RPC reads against OOM, reusing existing repo helpers, with a >16 MiB fail-closed test that does not touch shared test mocks._

> PR body follows the live `.github/pull_request_template.md` (What Problem / Why / User Impact / Evidence). Security & Privacy and Compatibility notes are folded into the relevant sections below.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where an operator running OpenClaw's Signal channel in **container mode** (the bbernhard `signal-cli-rest-api` container) could have the OpenClaw runtime's memory exhausted by a single oversized or runaway HTTP response from that container.

The Signal container is an external, user-configured HTTP dependency that OpenClaw does not control. The central RPC helper `containerRestRequest` read both the error body and the success body with `res.text()`, which buffers the **entire** response into memory before parsing. A hostile, compromised, or simply buggy container that streams an unbounded body could push the runtime into an out-of-memory condition. Because `send`, `sendTyping`, `sendReceipt`, `sendReaction`/`removeReaction`, and `version` all funnel through `containerRestRequest`, every container-mode Signal operation was exposed — not just attachment downloads (which were already bounded).

## Why This Change Was Made

Both reads in `containerRestRequest` are now bounded using the same readers already used elsewhere in this file and across the provider stack, so the fix adds no new size-limit machinery:

- **Success body:** `readProviderTextResponse(res, "Signal REST")` — reads under the shared 16 MiB provider cap (`PROVIDER_TEXT_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES`) and **fails closed** (rejects, cancelling the stream) on overflow. The existing empty-body → `undefined` and `JSON.parse` semantics are preserved exactly (read text, return `undefined` if empty, then `JSON.parse`).
- **Error body:** `readResponseTextLimited(res)` — reads only a bounded diagnostic prefix for the thrown error message instead of the full body. This matches how the shared provider error path and other extensions (openrouter/chutes/minimax/qqbot) already read error bodies.

This mirrors the attachment path in the same file (`readCappedResponseBuffer` → `readResponseWithLimit`) and the JSON readers in firecrawl / tavily / nextcloud-talk. Non-goal: this PR does not change protocol behavior, timeouts, or the 16 MiB cap value; it only changes how the body bytes are read.

**Compatibility:** No public API, config, or behavior change for well-behaved containers. Same return values, same error strings for normal-sized responses, same `undefined`-on-empty and `JSON.parse`-on-malformed behavior. The only observable difference is that a pathologically large body now produces a bounded error instead of unbounded buffering. No schema, CLI, or migration impact.

## User Impact

Operators running the Signal channel in container mode are no longer exposed to an OOM/denial-of-service vector from their `signal-cli-rest-api` endpoint. A malformed or oversized response now fails the single affected operation with a clear bounded error instead of risking the whole runtime. No action required from users; existing setups behave identically for normal responses.

**Security & Privacy:** This is a defense-in-depth hardening of an untrusted external boundary (the container response). It reduces a memory-exhaustion DoS surface. No new data is logged or transmitted; the error path now logs *less* (a bounded prefix rather than the full body). No secrets or PII handling changes.

## Evidence

Focused test run (config auto-selected `test/vitest/vitest.extension-signal.config.ts`):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/signal/src/client-container.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  51 passed (51)
```

The two new tests drive the **real** `containerRestRequest` (the readers run real; only `fetch` is mocked via this test file's existing local mock):

```
✓ containerRestRequest edge cases > fails closed when the success body exceeds the response size cap  43ms
✓ containerRestRequest edge cases > bounds the error body so a huge failure response cannot be buffered whole  35ms
```

- **Fail-closed proof:** a lazy pull-based `ReadableStream` emits 1 MiB chunks up to 20 MiB; the call rejects with `/exceeds \d+ bytes/` (`Signal REST: text response exceeds 16777216 bytes`) and asserts `emitted < 20`, proving the stream was cancelled at the 16 MiB cap rather than drained — i.e. no full buffering.
- **Error-body bound proof:** a 1 MiB error body yields a thrown message that starts with `Signal REST 500:` and is `< 64 KiB`, proving the full payload was not buffered into the error.

Test-isolation note: the new tests use only this file's local `fetch` mock plus a local `bodyStream` helper. The shared provider HTTP test mocks were intentionally **not** modified, to avoid cross-extension blast radius.

Type-check: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json` exits 0.
target.author: alix-007
